items.q: What is the worst-case complexity of bubble sort?
items.misconception: O(n) because it just passes through the list.
items.why: Students count one pass and forget that bubble sort does up to n passes, each comparing up to n elements. The nested loop structure gives n times n = n squared.

items.q: What is the denary value of binary 10110?
items.why: Students forget to apply place values. Each position is a power of 2 from right (1, 2, 4, 8, 16...). Reading binary as denary is the most common error.

items.q: What is the difference between OR and XOR?
items.correct: OR is true when at least one input is true; XOR is true when exactly one input is true.
items.misconception: They are the same gate with different names.
items.random_wrong: OR is for numbers, XOR is for letters.
items.why: Students miss the critical case: when BOTH inputs are 1. OR gives 1, XOR gives 0. XOR is 'exclusive' - excludes the both-true case.
It's Bubble sort. “Merge sort” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If you'll search 1,000,000 times, the cost of sorting first is:
items.why: It's Worth it for binary search. “Wasted” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Worst-case comparisons for binary search of 256 items? (log base 2)
items.why: Exact answer: 8. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
